message.content: Write a single very long sentence on one line. Make it at least 5000 words. Do not use newlines except at the end.

attachment.content:
- seo-plan
- seo-competitor-pages
- seo-technical
- seo-audit
- seo-images
- seo-hreflang
- seo-sitemap
- seo-content
- seo-page
- find-skills
- seo-programmatic
- seo-geo
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Claude's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations.
- session-postmortem
- ingo:triage-issue: Triage GitHub issues for the interplay repo. Use when asked to triage, review, or process open issues. Reads issue details, checks for duplicates, classifies as bug or feature, applies labels (bug/enhancement, Priority-High/Medium/Low, track:*), and posts a spec-ready triage comment.
- ingo:cka-coach
- frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications (examples include websites, landing pages, dashboards, React components, HTML/CSS layouts, or when styling/beautifying any web UI). Generates creative, polished code and UI design that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- seo: Comprehensive SEO analysis for any website or business type. Performs full site audits, single-page deep analysis, technical SEO checks (crawlability, indexability, Core Web Vitals with INP), schema markup detection/validation/generation, content quality assessment (E-E-A-T framework per Dec 2025 update extending to all competitive queries), image optimization, sitemap analysis, and Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) for AI Overviews, ChatGPT, and Perplexity citations. Analyzes AI crawler accessibility (GPTBot, ClaudeBot, PerplexityBot), llms.txt compliance, brand mention signals, and passage-level citability. Industry detection for SaaS, e-commerce, local business, publishers, agencies. Triggers on: "SEO", "audit", "schema", "Core Web Vitals", "sitemap", "E-E-A-T", "AI Overviews", "GEO", "technical SEO", "content quality", "page speed", "structured data".
- seo-schema
- expose-writer
- ir:agent-landscape: Scan the web for coding agents and agent orchestrators, track GitHub stars and trends, rank by popularity+momentum, and publish a report to the irrlicht site. Shows which agents irrlicht already supports. Use when user says 'agent landscape', 'scan agents', 'coding agent tracker', 'agent popularity', '/ir:agent-landscape', or wants to see the competitive landscape of coding agents.
- ir:exec: Investigate a GitHub issue and produce an implementation plan. Use at the start of a new task in a fresh worktree. Triggers on "/ir:exec", "plan issue", "plan this issue", "investigate issue", or when the user provides an issue number/URL and asks for a plan.
- ir:release: Build and publish an irrlicht release. Bumps version, builds Go daemon + Swift app, creates signed app bundle with icon, packages DMG (branded installer) + PKG, updates docs/changelog/landing page, commits, tags, pushes, and creates GitHub release with assets. Default: patch bump. Use "/ir:release minor" or "/ir:release major".
- ir:triage: Autonomously triage GitHub issues on ingo-eichhorst/Irrlicht. Diagnostic-only: scores each issue against a 6-axis readiness rubric and lands it at ready-for-agent or needs-info, with a one-line justification per label. Triggers: "/ir:triage" (sweep), "/ir:triage #N" (single), "/ir:triage #N #M".
- ir:onboard-agent: Produce the canonical scenario × adapter fixture matrix for irrlicht. Drives the real `claude` CLI (and future codex/pi drivers) through a shared scenario catalogue, records lifecycle events, stages curated fixtures under `.build/refresh/`, and summarizes material changes vs. committed fixtures. Unifies three operations: refresh, bootstrap, and new-agent onboarding. Use when user says "/ir:onboard-agent", "refresh fixtures", "onboard agent", "regenerate recordings", or "update replay fixtures".
- ir:test-sub-fg: Test foreground subagent detection in irrlicht. Launches 6 foreground Explore agents in 2 waves (3 + 3). Use when the user says "test foreground", "test fg", or "/ir:test-fg".
- ir:agent-releases: Check latest releases of coding agents monitored by irrlicht (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Pi, Gas Town) and report new features that impact session monitoring. Use when user says 'agent releases', 'check releases', 'agent updates', '/ir:agent-releases', or wants to know if upstream agent changes affect irrlicht.
- ir:test-mac: Build and restart the irrlicht daemon and macOS Swift app for local testing. Compiles the Go daemon, builds the Swift app, kills all running instances (including production Irrlicht.app), and launches the freshly built versions. Use when the user says "test mac", "restart mac", "rebuild mac", or "/ir:test-mac".
- ir:refresh-aliases
- ir:test-sub-bg: Test background agent detection in irrlicht. Launches 6 background Explore agents in 2 waves (3 + 3). Use when the user says "test background", "test bg", or "/ir:test-bg".
- claude-md-management:revise-claude-md: Update CLAUDE.md with learnings from this session
- claude-md-management:claude-md-improver
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review

content.text: In the beginning, before the first light had ever touched the surface of anything that could be called a world, before the concept of time had crystallized into the ticking, relentless, forward-marching thing it would eventually become, before even the notion of "before" had any meaning whatsoever, there existed a kind of potential, a vast and shimmering sea of possibility that contained within its depths every story that would ever be told, every mountain that would ever be climbed, every ocean that would ever be crossed by wooden ships with billowing sails carrying sailors who dreamed of distant shores and the smell of spices and the faces of people they had left behind in small harbors where children played and mothers watched the horizon and fathers built boats that would carry their sons away into the unknown, and it was from this primordial potential that the universe eventually chose to erupt in a blinding, incomprehensible instant of creation that scattered matter and energy across dimensions that had no names yet, because names require minds, and minds require brains, and brains require chemistry, and chemistry requires elements, and elements require stars, and stars require gravity, and gravity requires mass, and mass requires the kind of universe that had just, in that singular impossible moment, decided to exist, spinning out from nothing into everything with a violence and a grandeur that no language ever invented by any creature on any world in any galaxy would ever fully capture, though many would try, in paintings and poems and symphonies and equations scrawled on blackboards in rooms lit by candlelight where scholars with ink-stained fingers muttered to themselves and forgot to eat and fell asleep with their heads on books that contained the accumulated wisdom of generations of other scholars who had also forgotten to eat and fallen asleep on books, passing forward through the centuries this fragile, luminous thing called understanding, this attempt to hold the universe in the cup of a human mind and say, yes, I see you, I know something about you, I have measured the distance between stars and the weight of atoms and the speed at which light travels through the vacuum of space, which is a number so large it seems like an abstraction but is in fact the very real limit of how fast information can travel, which means that when you look at the night sky you are not seeing the universe as it is but as it was, you are looking backward through time at light that left its source thousands or millions or billions of years ago, when the stars that sent it may have already burned themselves to cinders and collapsed into themselves and become something else entirely, a white dwarf perhaps, or a neutron star, or in the grandest and most dramatic cases a black hole, which is not a hole at all but a region of spacetime where gravity has become so intense that not even light can escape its pull, which is a concept so strange and so counterintuitive that even the people who understand the mathematics of it admit that their intuitions are helpless in the face of it, because our intuitions were built for a world of medium-sized objects moving at medium speeds under conditions that are nowhere near extreme enough to reveal the true strangeness of reality, which is a strangeness that goes all the way down, past the atoms, past the protons and neutrons and electrons, past the quarks and the gluons, into a realm where particles are also waves and waves are also particles and the act of observation changes what is observed and things that are separated by vast distances can be entangled in ways that seem to violate every common-sense notion of how the world works, and all of this, every quark, every photon, every galaxy cluster spinning slowly in the dark, every ocean wave and butterfly wing and human heartbeat, is part of the same story, the story of a universe that came from nothing and is going somewhere, though where exactly is a question that physicists and philosophers and theologians have argued about for as long as there have been physicists and philosophers and theologians, which is to say for as long as there have been humans capable of looking up at the sky and wondering, and the wondering itself is perhaps the most remarkable thing of all, that the universe has produced beings who can contemplate the universe, who can sit in a field on a summer night and watch the stars wheel overhead and feel something that has no precise name but contains within it awe and longing and a sense of connection to something vast and ancient and indifferent and perhaps also beautiful, if beauty is something the universe intends or if it is only something we bring to the universe, projecting our own responses onto patterns of matter and energy that have no interest in being beautiful or ugly or anything at all, which is a question that artists and scientists have circled around for centuries without reaching agreement, though the circling itself has produced an extraordinary amount of beauty, in the form of paintings and sculptures and cathedrals and mathematical proofs and scientific theories and pieces of music that seem to capture something essential about the human condition, which is the condition of being alive and conscious and curious and mortal in a universe that is alive in some sense and conscious in no sense and curious about nothing and immortal only in the sense that it will outlast every individual thing within it, including the stars themselves, including the very atoms that make up the stars, if the theories are right about proton decay, and after the last proton has decayed and the last black hole has evaporated in a whisper of Hawking radiation and the universe has expanded to such a degree that nothing is anywhere near anything else and the temperature everywhere has dropped to within a fraction of a degree of absolute zero, after all of that, the universe will continue in a state of perfect stillness and perfect cold and perfect darkness for a duration so long that the age of the universe from its beginning to now would be an unimaginably small fraction of it, a rounding error, a moment so brief it would not register on any scale meaningful to the kind of beings who like to measure things, and yet here we are, in that brief moment, in that rounding error, writing poems and building cities and falling in love and grieving and laughing and eating bread that was baked by someone who woke up before dawn to start the ovens, someone who knows the weight of dough and the smell of yeast and the satisfaction of a good loaf pulled golden from the heat, someone who is part of a tradition of bakers stretching back thousands of years to the first human who discovered that grain ground and mixed with water and heated becomes something new, something that nourishes, something that can be shared, and the sharing of food is itself a kind of language, a way of saying I acknowledge your hunger, I acknowledge your existence, here is something I made with my hands, take it, and this simple transaction, repeated billions of times a day across the surface of the planet, is the fabric of human civilization, which is a strange and fragile and magnificent thing that appeared very recently on a small rocky planet orbiting an unremarkable star in one arm of a galaxy that contains hundreds of billions of stars, in a universe that contains hundreds of billions of galaxies, and yet from within this civilization, from within these small and temporary lives, human beings have managed to ask the right questions and develop the right tools and exercise the right kind of patient, persistent curiosity to figure out, at least roughly, how large the universe is and how old it is and what it is made of and how it began and how it will likely end, which is an achievement so extraordinary that it is difficult to convey without resorting to superlatives, because what other creature has ever looked at the stars and determined their composition by analyzing the light they emit, or measured the speed of their recession and from that inferred the expansion of space itself, or detected the ripples in spacetime caused by the collision of black holes a billion light years away, using instruments sensitive enough to measure a displacement smaller than a proton, which is itself a thing so small that if you scaled a proton up to the size of a marble, an atom would be the size of a football stadium, and yet somehow we know things about protons, we have mapped their internal structure, we have smashed them together at nearly the speed of light and watched what falls out of the collision and built from those observations a theory of fundamental physics that is the most precisely tested and verified theory in the history of science, correct to so many decimal places that if you scaled it up to predict the distance from New York to Los Angeles it would be accurate to within a fraction of a millimeter, and this theory, for all its precision, is known to be incomplete, because it doesn't account for gravity in a way that is consistent with the theory of general relativity, and both of these theories are known to break down in the extreme conditions of the very early universe and inside black holes, which means there is something deeper still to be discovered, some more fundamental framework that contains both theories as special cases, the way Newtonian mechanics is a special case of relativistic mechanics that works perfectly well when things are moving slowly compared to the speed of light, and finding this deeper theory is one of the great unsolved problems of physics, has been for a century, has occupied some of the finest minds of the twentieth and twenty-first centuries, and has so far resisted all attempts to crack it open, which is either frustrating or thrilling depending on your temperament, because it means there is still something genuinely unknown, still a frontier, still a place where human knowledge runs out and beyond which lies territory that no one has ever mapped, and the existence of that frontier is, in a certain light, the most encouraging thing imaginable, a reminder that the universe is under no obligation to make itself easy to understand, that it will continue to surprise us, that the map will never be complete, that there will always be another question hiding inside the answer to the previous question, like a set of nested dolls that gets smaller and smaller and never reaches a final smallest doll but just keeps going inward forever, which may be maddening or may be glorious, may be the thing that makes existence feel unbearably open-ended or the thing that makes it feel inexhaustibly rich, depending on the day and the light and the particular configuration of hopes and fears that you bring to the question, and everyone brings a different configuration because everyone has lived a different life, has been shaped by different experiences, different losses, different moments of unexpected joy, different encounters with beauty or with cruelty, different books read at different ages, different conversations had in different languages in different cities in different seasons, and all of these differences are what make the human project so complicated and so interesting, because there is no single human experience, there are billions of human experiences, each one unique and irreplaceable, each one containing within it the full complexity of what it is to be conscious and mortal and connected to other conscious mortal beings through bonds of language and love and shared history and mutual dependence, and the miracle is not that we understand each other as well as we do, the miracle is that we understand each other at all, that the sounds that come out of one person's mouth can enter another person's ears and cause something analogous to happen in another mind, that words can carry meaning across the gap between one consciousness and another, that a poem written in grief can be read centuries later by someone who has never met the poet and cause them to feel something true and real, that music composed in one country can be played in another and move people who share none of the composer's cultural context because there is something in the structure of music that speaks to something in the structure of human feeling that is deeper than culture, wired into us at some level that predates language and civilization, going back to a time before we were quite human, when our ancestors made sounds around fires to communicate something that words couldn't carry, and those fires lit up the dark of the African savanna and the faces of people who were recognizably us but also not quite us, on the edge of becoming us, developing the tools and the social structures and the symbolic thinking that would eventually produce everything that came after, all the cities and ships and libraries and hospitals and concert halls and laboratories, all the bread and the poetry and the cathedrals and the equations, all the love stories and the wars and the peace treaties and the revolutions and the slow grinding work of building something better than what was there before, which is never finished and never will be finished because the world keeps changing and the problems keep changing and the solutions to one problem always create new problems that require new solutions in a process that has no endpoint but also no ceiling, that can always get better, always become more just and more kind and more beautiful, if enough people want it to badly enough and are willing to do the difficult, unglamorous, patient, persistent work of making it so, work that happens mostly in small rooms by people whose names are not remembered, who never gave a famous speech or led a famous march but who showed up day after day and did what needed to be done and made it slightly easier for the people who came after them to do the same, and this is how progress actually works, not in dramatic leaps but in the accumulated weight of countless small acts of courage and decency and competence, the way a river carves a canyon not in a single violent event but through the patient, relentless, millimeter-by-millimeter wearing away of stone across millions of years, and the canyon that results is magnificent, is a testament to the power of persistence, and if you stand at the rim and look down at the layers of rock that have been exposed, each layer representing millions of years of history, each one a different color and texture recording different conditions in a world long gone, you can feel the depth of time in a way that is almost physical, a vertigo that is not just about the height of the cliff but about the height of the years, the sheer unfathomable depth of the past, and that depth is everywhere once you know how to look for it, in the mountains that were once ocean floors and the deserts that were once forests and the cities built on the ruins of older cities built on the ruins of older cities still, layer upon layer of human effort and human dreaming stretching down into the earth, and somewhere in those layers are the things we made that lasted, the tools and the texts and the bones, the traces that allow us to reconstruct something of what life was like for people who lived so long ago that their names are lost but their fingerprints are still, in some cases, preserved in the clay of the pots they shaped, and to press your own finger into the indentation left by a potter who lived five thousand years ago is to close a circuit across time, to touch someone across an abyss that is almost incomprehensible, to feel something of what connects all human beings to each other across all the centuries and all the distances, which is the simple fact that we are all the same kind of creature, driven by the same basic hungers and the same basic fears, capable of the same basic kindnesses and the same basic cruelties, always trying to make sense of a world that exceeds our capacity to make sense of it, always building something, always reaching for something just beyond our grasp, always loving people who will die and grieving people who have died and bringing new people into the world who will love and grieve in their turn, this endless cycle of presence and absence, arrival and departure, the world constantly replenishing itself with new beings who have no memory of what came before and must learn everything from scratch, which is both a tragedy and a gift, a tragedy because so much is lost with every generation and a gift because every generation gets to encounter the world with fresh eyes, to be surprised by things that have surprised every generation before them, to feel for the first time the particular shock and delight of falling in love or seeing the ocean or understanding for the first time how big the universe is, and these first times are irreplaceable, are perhaps the most vivid and most alive moments in any life, the moments when something enters you that changes the shape of you permanently, and you are never again exactly who you were before that moment, and the accumulation of those moments is what makes a life, what gives it its particular texture and color and weight, and no two accumulations are alike, which is why no two lives are alike, and why the project of understanding other people is infinite and why literature exists and why we keep telling stories even though we have been telling stories since before we had writing to preserve them, telling stories around fires and under stars and in the dark before sleep, telling stories to make sense of what has happened and to prepare for what might happen and to feel less alone in a universe that offers no guarantees and no explanations and no comfort except what we make for each other, which turns out, against all odds, to be enough, to be more than enough, to be the thing that makes everything else bearable and sometimes even beautiful, this human warmth, this improbable grace.

lastPrompt: Write a single very long sentence on one line. Make it at least 5000 words. Do not use newlines except at the end.
Write a single very long sentence on one line. Make it at least 5000 words. Do not use newlines except at the end.
